questions.question_text: When must a Kansas licensee provide the BRRETA brokerage relationship brochure to a prospective buyer?
distractors: At the time of contract signing
Within 48 hours of first contact
After the buyer makes an offer
questions.explanation: K.S.A. 58-30,110 requires licensees to furnish prospective buyers and sellers with the brokerage relationships brochure at the first practical opportunity. Receipt must later be acknowledged by the buyer and seller in any contract for sale.

questions.question_text: A licensee meets a prospective seller at the seller's home, tours the property, and discusses pricing strategy, never presenting the brokerage relationships brochure. Has the licensee met the BRRETA disclosure requirement?
questions.correct_answer: No, it was due at first practical opportunity
distractors: Yes, pricing may be discussed before disclosure
Yes, disclosure is only required for buyers
No, disclosure is due after signing the listing
questions.explanation: K.S.A. 58-30,110(a)(2) requires licensees to furnish a prospective seller with the brokerage relationships brochure at the first practical opportunity. A full in-person listing consultation is past that point, so the requirement was missed.

questions.question_text: A buyer walks into an open house. At what point must the hosting agent provide brokerage relationship information?
questions.correct_answer: At the first practical opportunity in the visit
distractors: After the buyer submits a written offer
Only if the buyer requests representation
When the buyer signs a purchase agreement
questions.explanation: K.S.A. 58-30,110(a)(2) requires the brochure at the first practical opportunity, which at an open house means early in the interaction with a prospective buyer — not waiting until an offer or a purchase agreement.

questions.question_text: The BRRETA brochure informs consumers about which of the following?
distractors: Commission rates charged by licensees
Property inspection requirements
Mortgage lending options in Kansas
questions.explanation: The BRRETA brochure explains the types of brokerage relationships available in Kansas (seller agency, buyer agency, transaction brokerage, designated agency) so consumers can make informed choices.

questions.question_text: A licensee provides the BRRETA brochure to a buyer after showing three properties and discussing each in detail. What is the consequence?
questions.correct_answer: The licensee violated the timing requirement
distractors: The disclosure is still valid and timely
The buyer automatically becomes a client
The transaction must be voided entirely
questions.explanation: The brochure must be furnished at the first practical opportunity under K.S.A. 58-30,110(a)(2), and waiting until after showing and discussing three properties is well past that point. The late disclosure violates BRRETA but does not void the transaction (K.S.A. 58-30,101(b)).

questions.question_text: What type of agreement is required to establish an agency relationship in Kansas?
distractors: A verbal agreement with witnesses
An implied agreement through conduct
A notarized commission contract
questions.explanation: Under BRRETA, all agency agreements must be in writing. Without a written agreement, the default transaction brokerage relationship applies, and no agency exists.

questions.question_text: A licensee verbally agrees to represent a buyer and begins showing properties. Is this a valid agency relationship?
questions.correct_answer: No, a written agreement is required for agency
distractors: Yes, verbal agreements create agency
Yes, if both parties acknowledge it
Yes, showing property implies agency
questions.explanation: Kansas law requires agency agreements to be in writing. A verbal agreement does not establish agency; the relationship defaults to transaction brokerage under BRRETA.

questions.question_text: Under BRRETA, how may a broker be engaged as a transaction broker?
questions.correct_answer: By either an oral or a written agreement
distractors: Only by a written and signed agreement
Only by conduct implying representation
By a consent form filed with KREC
questions.explanation: K.S.A. 58-30,103(c) allows a broker to be engaged as a transaction broker by oral or written agreement, and transaction brokerage is also the default relationship when no agency is established. Only agency relationships require express written authority.

questions.question_text: A seller signs a listing agreement that does not specify the type of brokerage relationship. What relationship is presumed?
distractors: Seller agency by default
Dual agency for all parties
No relationship until clarified
questions.explanation: If the written agreement does not clearly establish agency, the BRRETA default of transaction brokerage applies. A specific written agency agreement is needed to create an agency relationship.

questions.question_text: Why does Kansas require agency agreements to be in writing?
questions.correct_answer: To clearly define duties owed to each party
distractors: To calculate the correct commission rate
To satisfy federal lending requirements
To register the transaction with the state
questions.explanation: Agency exists only by express written authority (K.S.A. 58-30,102), and the written agreement must state its terms, expiration date, compensation, and the statutory duties owed (K.S.A. 58-30,103(f)). This ensures the client understands the representation; transaction brokerage, by contrast, may be oral or written.

questions.question_text: Where must the brokerage relationship be disclosed in a Kansas real estate transaction?
questions.correct_answer: In any contract for sale of property
distractors: Only in the listing agreement
In a separate disclosure filed with KREC
In the closing statement only
questions.explanation: Kansas requires that the brokerage relationship be disclosed in any contract for sale. This ensures all parties to the purchase agreement know each licensee's role in the transaction.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent and a transaction broker are involved in the same sale. What must the purchase contract reveal about their roles?
questions.correct_answer: Each licensee's relationship type with parties
distractors: Only the buyer's agent relationship
Only the commission split arrangement
Nothing unless a party requests it
questions.explanation: The purchase contract must disclose each licensee's role. All parties must be informed whether a licensee is acting as a buyer's agent, seller's agent, or transaction broker.

questions.question_text: A purchase agreement is signed but fails to disclose the brokerage relationships. What issue does this create?
questions.correct_answer: A violation of Kansas disclosure requirements
distractors: The contract is automatically void
No issue if parties know their agents
The title company will add it at closing
questions.explanation: Kansas requires disclosure of brokerage relationships in the contract for sale. Omitting this disclosure violates BRRETA requirements and may expose the licensee to disciplinary action.

questions.question_text: In a Kansas transaction, who must be informed of each licensee's brokerage role?
distractors: Only the party the licensee represents
Only the buyer in every transaction
Only the closing attorney or title company
questions.explanation: All parties in the transaction must be informed of each licensee's role. This transparency ensures every party understands who represents whom and what duties are owed.

questions.question_text: A designated agent for the seller drafts a purchase contract. The agent lists only the seller's brokerage relationship and omits the buyer's agent's role. Is this acceptable?
questions.correct_answer: No, all licensee roles must be disclosed
distractors: Yes, each agent discloses their own role
Yes, the buyer's agent adds it later
Only if the buyer waives the requirement
questions.explanation: The purchase contract must disclose all brokerage relationships involved in the transaction. Omitting the buyer's agent's role fails to meet the disclosure requirement under Kansas law.
